flashcards.back: Transmission media is the physical pathway or communication channel through which data travels from sender to receiver in a network. It can be guided (wired) or unguided (wireless).

flashcards.front: List the two main categories of transmission media.
flashcards.back:
1. Guided Media (bounded/wired): Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Fiber Optic
2. Unguided Media (wireless): Radio Waves, Microwave, Infrared, Satellite
Twisted pair consists of two copper wires twisted together in pairs to reduce electromagnetic interference (EMI). Used in telephone and Ethernet networks.
UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair): No shielding, cheaper, susceptible to EMI. STP (Shielded Twisted Pair): Has metallic shielding, better EMI protection, more expensive.
Coaxial cable has a central copper conductor surrounded by insulation, braided metal shielding, and outer jacket. Used for TV distribution and high-speed data transmission.

flashcards.front: Name the two types of Coaxial cables commonly used.
flashcards.back:
1. Thinnet (10Base2): Thin, flexible, up to 185m, used in LANs
2. Thicknet (10Base5): Thicker, more durable, up to 500m, used for backbone cabling
Fiber optic cable transmits data as light pulses through thin glass or plastic fibers. It offers high bandwidth, low attenuation, and immunity to EMI.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between Single Mode and Multi-Mode fiber.
flashcards.back: Single Mode: Core diameter 8-10μm, one light path, long distance (up to 100km), used in telco. Multi-Mode: Core 50-62.5μm, multiple light paths, short distance (up to 2km), used in LANs.

flashcards.front: What is attenuation in transmission media?
flashcards.back: Attenuation is the loss of signal strength as it travels through the medium. Measured in decibels (dB). Repeaters or amplifiers are used to boost signals.
EMI is external electromagnetic signals that interfere with data transmission, causing data corruption. Shielded cables help reduce EMI.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of Fiber Optic over Copper?
flashcards.back:
1. Higher bandwidth
2. Lower attenuation
3. Immune to EMI
4. Better security (difficult to tap)
5. Lighter weight
6. Longer distances without repeaters
Microwave uses high-frequency radio waves (1-300 GHz) for point-to-point communication. Requires line-of-sight between antennas. Used in telephone and data networks.
Satellite communication uses artificial satellites orbiting Earth to relay microwave signals. Used for TV broadcasting, telephone, and internet across vast distances.
Infrared uses infrared light waves for short-range, line-of-sight communication. Used in TV remotes, mobile phones, and wireless keyboards. Cannot penetrate walls.

flashcards.front: What determines the choice of transmission medium?
flashcards.back: Factors: Bandwidth requirement, distance, cost, immunity to interference, installation complexity, security needs, and environmental conditions.
